title: Prefer composition over boolean prop combinations
principle: Model meaningful component parts and mutually exclusive variants explicitly; avoid APIs whose independent flags create invalid visual and behavioral combinations.
rationale: Boolean growth multiplies states, hides intent at call sites, and makes test coverage and accessibility guarantees unmanageable.
applies_when: A reusable component has multiple optional regions, modes, or behaviors.
exceptions: A genuinely independent binary capability may remain a boolean when its interaction with every variant is defined.
implementation: Use children, slots, subcomponents, discriminated variants, or separate components.
Reject invalid combinations in types and runtime guards where appropriate.
verification: The public API reads like the design intent.
The state matrix contains no reachable unsupported combinations.
accessibility: Semantic and keyboard behavior is owned by the primitive and cannot be disabled accidentally through composition.
responsive: Responsive adaptation occurs inside the contract or through explicit composition, not hidden prop magic.
performance: Composition does not force all optional behavior into the base bundle.
browser_notes: Framework abstraction retains native element behavior.

title: Place the server-client boundary intentionally
principle: Render stable content and data near the server, and move only stateful browser interaction across the client boundary with serializable, minimal props.
rationale: Accidental client boundaries expand bundles, duplicate fetching, expose server concerns, and create hydration delay for content that did not need interactivity.
applies_when: Using server rendering, React Server Components, hydration, or mixed rendering.
exceptions: A genuinely offline-first or highly local interactive tool may justify a broader client boundary.
implementation: Keep secrets and privileged data access server-side and pass derived display data.
verification: Review route bundles and component boundaries.
Content does not refetch or flash unnecessarily after hydration.
accessibility: Server-rendered semantics are usable before hydration and client transitions preserve focus.
responsive: Server markup supports every layout without user-agent-specific duplicate trees.
performance: Measure client JavaScript, hydration work, caching, and request waterfalls.
browser_notes: Hydration mismatches and unsupported browser-only API access are caught in testing.

title: Build a useful baseline before enhancement
principle: Deliver semantic content and core task behavior with robust platform primitives, then enhance when APIs, script, network, and user preferences permit.
rationale: Web execution is partial and variable; enhancement limits the blast radius of blocked code, unsupported APIs, and slow networks.
applies_when: A task can be expressed through links, forms, disclosure, media, or document structure.
exceptions: A computation-heavy application may require script but still needs a clear loading, failure, and support path.
implementation: Start with HTML navigation and submission semantics.
Gate enhancements with feature detection and preserve URLs, history, and recoverability.
verification: Block optional script or APIs and inspect the baseline.
Enhancement failure never traps the user in a half-active state.
accessibility: Baseline semantics establish name, role, value, and keyboard access.
responsive: Core content reflows before advanced layout enhancements apply.
performance: Useful content arrives before optional enhancement payloads.
browser_notes: Use capability detection and test baseline browsers directly.

title: Give state one clear owner
principle: Assign each piece of durable state to a canonical owner—URL, server, form, component, or external store—and derive rather than duplicate it.
rationale: Duplicated state drifts across controls, visuals, accessibility attributes, history, and server responses, especially during async updates.
applies_when: State affects navigation, sharing, persistence, validation, selection, or multiple components.
exceptions: A local optimistic snapshot may temporarily diverge when reconciliation and rollback are explicit.
implementation: Classify state by lifetime and sharing scope.
Use URLs for shareable view state and server data as the authority for persisted records.
verification: Refresh, back/forward, deep link, retry, and concurrent update produce predictable state.
Visual and ARIA state derive from the same source.
accessibility: Announcements, focus, and expanded/selected states update atomically with canonical state.
responsive: Alternate responsive controls manipulate the same state rather than synchronized copies.
performance: Derivations are profiled before memoization; avoid global stores for isolated state.
browser_notes: History and URL encoding behavior is tested across browsers.

title: Test representative component states as first-class artifacts
principle: Maintain deterministic fixtures for relevant states and test semantics, interaction, and high-value visual invariants rather than only the default snapshot.
rationale: Default-only snapshots miss the states most likely to regress and produce noisy diffs that reviewers learn to approve blindly.
applies_when: A component is shared, interactive, asynchronous, or visually critical.
exceptions: A static private fragment may rely on page-level coverage if its state cannot vary.
implementation: Use the shared state matrix to create stories or fixtures.
Assert behavior and accessibility; reserve image diffs for meaningful layout and styling risk.
verification: Every applicable state maps to a test or documented manual check.
Tests fail on a deliberate semantic, interaction, or visual regression.
accessibility: Automated accessibility checks are supplemented by keyboard and assistive-technology review for complex widgets.
responsive: Test boundary widths and content expansion, not only one desktop viewport.
performance: Heavy fixtures include render and interaction profiling where warranted.
browser_notes: High-risk platform behavior is promoted to cross-browser tests.

title: Scale browser testing by feature and user risk
principle: Map browser and device coverage to audience, capability risk, task consequence, and regression history rather than running every test everywhere.
rationale: A risk matrix concentrates expensive real-platform testing where differences can block users while retaining automated breadth elsewhere.
applies_when: Planning CI, release checks, device labs, or manual QA.
exceptions: A contractual certification matrix may impose additional fixed coverage.
implementation: Score critical flows and platform-sensitive features.
Assign unit, integration, automation, and real-device evidence proportionally.
verification: Every critical flow has a named browser/device path.
Escaped regressions feed back into matrix weights.
accessibility: Include representative assistive-technology pairs for complex and critical flows.
responsive: Cover minimum, transition, wide, zoomed, and virtual-keyboard conditions based on risk.
performance: Include at least one representative low-power device and constrained network path.
browser_notes: Review audience data and support policy before each major release.

title: Release visual and structural change incrementally
principle: Separate foundation, component, and page migrations into observable, reversible increments with compatibility boundaries.
rationale: Large redesign releases combine visual, behavioral, content, and infrastructure risk, making defects difficult to attribute and rollback.
applies_when: A change affects multiple routes, system primitives, or critical user flows.
exceptions: A security or legal deadline may require a coordinated cutover with a rehearsed rollback.
implementation: Use flags or route cohorts where appropriate, dual-run compatible tokens, and migration dashboards.
Define stop and rollback signals before exposure.
verification: Each increment has acceptance evidence and can be disabled without data loss.
Old and new experiences do not diverge in semantics or permissions.
accessibility: Run gates on every increment; do not defer accessibility until visual migration completes.
responsive: Rollout evidence covers supported viewport and input modes.
performance: Watch bundle duplication and field metrics during coexistence.
browser_notes: Compatibility layers follow the declared baseline until migration completes.

title: Use completion gates, not visual approval alone
principle: A frontend change is complete only after requirements, content, states, accessibility, responsiveness, performance, browser support, testing, and release evidence pass their applicable gates.
rationale: A screenshot can look finished while keyboard use, empty states, network failure, localization, or production delivery remains broken.
applies_when: Handing off or releasing any user-facing frontend work.
exceptions: A prototype may mark gates not applicable only when its non-production scope is explicit.
implementation: Use the corpus completion-gates artifact and attach evidence or a reasoned waiver.
Assign owners and expiry dates to waivers.
verification: No mandatory applicable gate remains unknown.
A reviewer can reproduce the evidence from documented commands or fixtures.
accessibility: Automated and manual accessibility evidence is included according to risk.
responsive: Continuous resizing and boundary-width evidence is included.
performance: Budgets are measured against representative production output.
browser_notes: Declared baseline and high-risk real-platform checks pass.
